sanskrit_devanagari:
या निशा सर्वभूतानां तस्यां जागर्ति संयमी ।
यस्यां जाग्रति भूतानि सा निशा पश्यतो मुनेः ॥ ६९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yā niśā sarva-bhūtānāṁ tasyāṁ jāgarti saṁyamī |
yasyāṁ jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśā paśyato muneḥ || 69 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (text body identical; punctuation differs — single danda)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.69।।)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.069 = MBh_06,024.069)

translations.verbatim_quote: What is night for all beings is the time of awakening for the self-controlled; and the time of awakening for all beings is night for the introspective sage.
What all beings consider as day is the night of ignorance for the wise, and what all creatures see as night is the day for the introspective sage.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are two classes of intelligent men. One is intelligent in material activities for sense gratification, and the other is introspective and awake to the cultivation of self-realization. Activities of the introspective sage, or thoughtful man, are night for persons materially absorbed.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The verse is not literal day-night reversal — it is a perception inversion. The sage's wakefulness is to the Self (or, in Gaudiya reading, the Lord); the ordinary being's wakefulness is to sense-objects. What absorbs the ordinary mind is darkness to the sage; what absorbs the sage is darkness to the ordinary mind. The verse is naming a real perceptual asymmetry, not asceticism.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Ya, what; is nisa, night — for all beings is the supreme Reality which is the object of vision of the man of realization, since just as in the night what exists is hidden from those who do not see, so too the supreme Reality is hidden from beings ignorant by nature; in that supreme Reality which is night for them, jagarti, is awake; the samyami, self-restrained, the man whose senses are controlled — that is, the yogi who has shaken off the sleep of ignorance. And conversely, yasyam, that ignorance — in which beings are awake, viz the perception of differentiated forms which is itself ignorance — sa nisa, that is night; pasyatah muneh, of the seeing sage, the man who has realized the highest Truth.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara's reading is precise: the inversion is between the supreme Reality (paramārtha-sat, the unitive ground) — to which the saṁyamī is awake and ordinary beings are asleep — and the field of differentiated forms (vyāvahārika) — to which ordinary beings are awake and the muni is asleep. The verse is not poetic license but a strict statement of what the sage's prajñā perceives versus what the ordinary mind's prajñā perceives. The same external scene admits two different perceptions; the verse names the inversion of which content fills the perception.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Self that is the field of meditation for the saṁyamī — that very Self is, for the worldly being absorbed in sense-objects, as if it were night, since they neither perceive nor pursue it. And conversely, the field of sense-objects in which worldly beings are wakeful — that field is night for the muni, who has turned from it as one closes the eyes against an irrelevant light.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja preserves the structural inversion (saṁyamī awake to A and asleep to B; ordinary beings awake to B and asleep to A) and adds the Vishishtadvaita specification: A is the Self as the field of meditation; B is the field of sense-objects. The closing image is striking — the muni 'closes the eyes against an irrelevant light' — preserving the verse's reading that this is not numbness but a redirected perceptual aperture.

positions.rendering: night of ignorance / day / introspective sage
night = the supreme Reality hidden from the ignorant; jāgarti = awake to that Reality; paśyataḥ = of the one who sees the highest Truth
night = the field opposed to one's chosen aperture; jāgarti = wakeful in the chosen field; paśyataḥ = of the one who has redirected perception toward the Self / Lord

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree the verse is a perceptual inversion, not a literal nocturnal practice. They differ on the content of what is awake-to. Shankara strictly: paramārtha-sat versus vyāvahārika. Ramanuja: the Self (as locus of devotion) versus sense-objects. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render less specifically — the worldly versus the introspective. Mukundananda makes one further move: he glosses the first 'night' as 'night of ignorance', adding interpretive content not strictly present in the Sanskrit. The Sanskrit just says niśā / day-night without explicit interpretive prefix.

positions.rendering: samyami = self-restrained, senses controlled; muneḥ = man who has realized the highest Truth
saṁyamī = the disciplined aspirant; muneḥ = the realized one

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: saṁyamī (from sam + yam, 'restrained on every side') and muniḥ (from man, 'one who reflects / is silent') are distinct technical terms. Shankara reads them as pointing to two stages — the saṁyamī is the practitioner of restraint, the muni is the realised one. Ramanuja preserves a similar distinction. English translators tend to flatten both to 'sage' or 'wise', which loses the chapter's careful technical vocabulary about the disciplined-aspirant-versus-realised continuum.

literal_meaning: What is night for all beings, in that the self-controlled one is awake; that in which beings are awake, that is night for the seeing sage.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a perceptual inversion. The same external scene admits two different fillings of perception. The saṁyamī (the self-controlled one) is awake to what ordinary beings sleep through — the substrate, the Self, the supreme Reality. Ordinary beings are awake to what the muni perceives only as night — the field of differentiated sense-objects and their pursuit. Shankara reads this strictly as the paramārtha versus vyāvahārika inversion. Ramanuja reads the inversion as Self-as-field-of-devotion versus sense-objects. Prabhupada (Gaudiya) reads the awake-content as the Lord rather than impersonal Self. All four traditions agree: the verse describes a real asymmetry of perception, not asceticism, not ironic poetry. The sage and the ordinary mind, looking at the same scene, fill its content with different objects of attention; what one sees fully the other does not see at all.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr. — paraphrase from JS-rendered page)
remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; only paraphrase recovered.
